column2: The dominant optimization channel (charge vs. capacitance) varies spatially with ε and d/R: low ε or large d/R → charge-limited (optimize contact area); high ε and small d/R → capacitance-limited (optimize dielectric thickness). Different regions of the parameter space require qualitatively different optimization strategies.
column3: Material selection and initial design scoping
column4: FOMS ∝ Qsc² × invC_sum decomposes into two independent physical channels. At low ε, Qsc² variance dominates total FOMS variation; at high ε, invC_sum contribution grows as dielectric enhancement saturates charge gains.
column5: Charge-dominant fraction: 62.0% (ε=1) → 52.8% (ε=10) at dd=0.125. Large dd amplifies charge dominance: 72.2% at dd=0.5 vs 58.3% at dd=0.125 (both ε=3). Capacitance-dominant rises from 9.3% to 27.8% as ε increases from 1 to 10.
column6: Global SHAP ranks features by average importance but cannot reveal that the same feature (e.g., hh) operates through different physical channels in different parameter regions.
column7: Fig.2 (mechanism phase diagram) + Fig.3 (design regime maps)

column2: Optimal dielectric thickness hh and sector count n are channel-dependent: in charge-limited regions, use thinner dielectrics (hh ≈ 0.008–0.016) and fewer sectors (n = 4–8); in capacitance-limited regions, use thicker dielectrics (hh ≈ 0.031–0.125) and more sectors (n = 16–64).
column3: Parameter tuning after identifying the dominant channel from Rule 1
column4: In charge-limited regions, thin dielectrics maximize contact charge while n saturates early due to edge-field crowding. In capacitance-limited regions, thicker dielectrics are tolerated because the capacitive penalty is offset by ε, and higher n continues to improve charge collection.
column5: Optimal hh shifts upward with ε across all tested (n, dd) combinations: e.g., at n=4/dd=0.125: ε=1→hh=0.031, ε=3→0.063, ε=10→0.125. Optimal n at dd=0.125/hh=0.016: ε=1→n=8, ε=10→n=16. At hh=0.008: ε=1→n=16, ε=10→n=64.
column6: SHAP provides one global ranking; it cannot give conditional recommendations like 'if ε < 3, set n ≤ 8; if ε > 7, n can extend to 32'.

column2: The mixed-dominance transition zone (20–36% of parameter space) offers the best balance between performance and robustness, as neither channel is saturated and both contribute to FOMS.
column3: Multi-objective optimization balancing performance and manufacturability
column4: In the mixed zone, FOMS responds to improvements in both channels simultaneously, providing moderate but stable performance. Pure charge-limited or capacitance-limited extremes yield higher peak FOMS but with diminishing returns from the saturated channel.
column5: Mixed regime occupies 19.4–28.7% across all 4 tested scenarios. Top-5% FOMS designs cluster at small dd (mean=0.056) and small hh (mean=0.040) — deep in single-channel-dominated territory — while moderate designs (top 5–30%) have dd mean=0.109, hh mean=0.081, closer to the mixed zone.
column6: Global feature importance cannot identify transition zones or map them spatially. The concept of a 'mixed regime' requires channel decomposition.

column2: High-FOMS designs and robust designs occupy opposite regions of the parameter space: the performance frontier (top 5%) has median CV > 10% under ±10% manufacturing tolerance, while designs at moderate FOMS (top 30%) can achieve CV < 5%. Specific safe-design thresholds depend on ε and d/R.
column3: Industrial manufacturing with realistic tolerances; tolerance-constrained optimization
column4: Extreme geometric ratios (small dd, small hh) that maximize FOMS also amplify relative perturbation effects. A ±10% variation in dd at dd=0.03 is only 0.003 in absolute terms but dramatically changes the electric field distribution.
column5: CV<5% safe zone: 40.7% of designs at ε=1/dd=0.125 but 0% at ε=3/dd=0.5. Worst-case retention minimum: 2.7% at ε=1/dd=0.125 (catastrophic loss). Median CV across scenarios: 6.8% (ε=1/dd=0.125) → 17.2% (ε=3/dd=0.5). Recommended: target top-30% FOMS with CV<5% for manufacturable designs.
column6: Global SHAP rankings and scalar performance summaries do not quantify this tolerance-aware trade-off or identify the corresponding robust candidate window under the present perturbation protocol.
